10.1016/j.jmp.2005.07.003

Gutiérrez-Peña and Walker (2001)  introduced a conceptually intuitive approach to model selection based on Bayesian nonparametric inference. Given available evidence  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               n

                               f 
                            
                            
                               nd

                               f 
                            
                            
                               0

                               0

                         f

                               x 
                            
                            
                               n

                               0 
                            
                         
                      , according to a nonparametric prior that gives positive support to all sampling densities

                         { 
                         f

                         ·

This article provides an extensive investigation of this Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model testing.  Section 2  motivates the Bayesian nonparametric approach on the basis of decision theory.  Section 3  discusses the advantages of this approach over all other approaches to model selection. That section also discusses the advantages of the approach over the traditional approaches to model testing, that are based on interpreting a  
                         p 
                      -value under the test of a null hypothesis  
                         
                            
                               H 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                         : 
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               nd 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       against the alternative  
                         
                            
                               H 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         : 
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               nd 
                            
                         
                         ≠ 
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                      .  Section 4  describes applications of the Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model testing, on four real data sets, in the evaluation of several models that are important to the practice of mathematical psychology. Finally,  Section 5  ends with conclusions.

Before proceeding to describe the Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model testing, it is necessary to provide a short introduction of the main concepts of Bayesian nonparametric statistical inference, and then to relate these concepts to parametric and deterministic models which are ubiquitous in scientific practice.

There are many different types of nonparametric priors that can be implemented for statistical inference. 
                            3 
                         
                         
                            3 
                            In principle, the Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model testing, discussed in this article, can be applied with any type of nonparametric prior. 
                          Many examples of such priors include Dirichlet Processes ( Ferguson, 1973 ), mixtures of Dirichlet Processes ( Antoniak, 1974; Lo, 1984 ), Pólya Trees ( Ferguson, 1974; Kraft, 1964 ;  Lavine, 1992, 1994 ;  Mauldin et al., 1992 ), generalized exponential Gaussian process priors ( Lenk, 1988, 1991 ), Bernstein polynomial priors ( Petrone, 1999a,b ;  Petrone & Wasserman, 2002 ), neutral priors ( Doksum, 1974; Hjort, 1990; Walker & Muliere, 1997 ), Dirichlet diffusion-tree priors ( Neal, 2003 ), quantile pyramids ( Hjort & Walker, 2004 ) and other priors ( Dubins & Freedman, 1967; Nieto-Barajas et al., 2004 ). For excellent reviews of nonparametric priors, see  Sinha and Dey (1997) ,  Dey et al. (1998) ,  Walker et al. (1999) ,  MacEachern and Müller (2000) ,  Ghosh and Ramamoorthi (2003) ,  Hjort (2003) , and  Müller and Quintana (2004) .

Finally, it is important to note that when 
                            5 
                         
                         
                            5 
                            de Finetti's General Representation Theorem may seem impressive in the sense that it guarantees the existence of a Bayesian nonparametric model describing the joint probability of the data  
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        n 
                                     
                                  
                               , simply when the data contains  
                                  m 
                                  ⩾ 
                                  1 
                                exchangeable sequences of observations. However, as discussed by  Walker and Damien (2000) , the Theorem does not guarantee that coherent statistical inferences will result from this model. Specifically, the chosen nonparametric prior  
                                  Π 
                                may give poor support to the true sampling density  
                                  
                                     
                                        f 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                               , resulting in a posterior distribution  
                                  
                                     
                                        Π 
                                     
                                     
                                        n 
                                     
                                  
                                that is inconsistent, such that the posterior probability  
                                  
                                     
                                        Π 
                                     
                                     
                                        n 
                                     
                                  
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        f 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                                
                               does not  converge to 1 as  
                                  n 
                                  → 
                                  ∞ 
                                (i.e.,  
                                  
                                     
                                        f 
                                     
                                     
                                        n 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  E 
                                  [ 
                                  f 
                                  
                                  | 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        n 
                                     
                                  
                                  ] 
                                does not converge to the true  
                                  
                                     
                                        f 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                                as  
                                  n 
                                  → 
                                  ∞ 
                               ). See  Diaconis and Freedman (1986)  for some dramatic examples of inconsistent posteriors that can arise from the Dirichlet Process prior, a type of nonparametric prior. 
                          a nonparametric prior distribution  
                            Π 
                          is appropriately specified to give positive support to the entire space of sampling densities  
                            
                               
                                  Ω 
                               
                               
                                  X 
                               
                            
                         , such that adequate support is given to the unknown true sampling density  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                          of a set of data  
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         , the resulting posterior distribution  
                            
                               
                                  Π 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                          is consistent in the sense that it converges to  
                            
                               
                                  Π 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                                  = 
                                  ∞ 
                               
                            
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                            = 
                            1 
                          as the sample size  n  approaches infinity. As a consequence, the point-estimate  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            E 
                            [ 
                            f 
                            
                            | 
                            
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                            ] 
                          converges to the true  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                          as  
                            n 
                            → 
                            ∞ 
                         . Thus, such a nonparametric prior distribution  
                            Π 
                          defines a “true” model that satisfies  posterior consistency , in the sense that as the sample size grows to  
                            ∞ 
                         , the model's posterior  
                            
                               
                                  Π 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                                  = 
                                  ∞ 
                               
                            
                          converges to giving total support to the (unknown) truth  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         .

It then follows that a model is defined as a true model if and only if the model satisfies posterior consistency ( Walker & Damien, 2000 ). For a full description of the necessary and sufficient conditions under which a prior distribution  
                            Π 
                          leads to a consistent posterior distribution  
                            
                               
                                  Π 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         , see  Walker (2004b)  and the important references therein.

Parametric and deterministic models are ubiquitous in the sciences, especially in mathematical psychology. It is thus natural to consider how such models relate to the framework of Bayesian nonparametric inference. The following two points define any parametric model and any deterministic model, respectively, from either a Bayesian and a classical-frequentist perspective of statistical inference.

                            13 
                            To give just one example from mathematical psychology, Multinomial Processing Tree (MPT) models provide a very important class of substantively-motivated psychological models ( Batchelder & Riefer, 1999 ). In such models, a subject's set of multinomial responses is assumed to arise from parameters  
                                  θ 
                                  = 
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        θ 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  … 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        θ 
                                     
                                     
                                        S

                                that characterize latent-cognitive processes, such as memory storage, memory retrieval, recognition, logical processes, and so forth. Although such parametric models are not strictly correct, they still can convey a lot of important information about the cognitive processes of humans. Interestingly, an MPT model can be specified to be correct, according to a Bayesian hierarchical modelling approach (e.g., see  Lo, 1984 ), in which the prior distribution  
                                  G 
                                  ( 
                                  θ 
                                  ) 
                                is defined at stage 1, and at stage 2, the prior distribution  G  is specified to be governed by a non-parametric prior distribution  
                                  Π 
                               . An MPT model has never been constructed in this way, but this should be done in future research. 
                          More generally, many researchers often consider among several “simple,” potentially-incorrect models, with (1) the intention of performing statistical inferences from the best model that is closest (in some sense) to the true sampling density  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0

                                  f

                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  n

                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         , and/or a procedure for  model testing  is used to decide whether a given model explains enough information about the (unknown) true  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         .

The next subsection presents a Bayesian nonparametric approach that can be used for making decisions that arise from the practice of model selection and model testing, based on ideas of Bayesian statistical decision theory.

Bayesian statistical inference arises as a mathematical consequence of the axioms of quantitative coherence, in which the aim is to find the optimal decision that maximizes expected utility (e.g.,  Bernardo & Smith, 1994 ;  Fishburn, 1981, 1986 ;  Savage, 1954 , and references therein; see also  Cox, 1946 ). Accordingly, the Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model testing consists of the following elements of decision:

                                                 1 
                                              
                                           
                                        . The locality requirement can be viewed as a likelihood principle for utility functions, because it requires the utility to depend only upon the probability density of the true state, and not upon the density of the states that could have obtained but did not. Furthermore, the logarithmic utility function provides the  only  proper local utility function that is smooth (continuously differentiable). 
                                   (see  Bernardo, 1979 ).

The locality requirement can be viewed as a likelihood principle for utility functions, because it requires the utility to depend only upon the probability density of the true state, and not upon the density of the states that could have obtained but did not.                          On the first point, consistency is a basic requirement for a model selection approach that provides coherent statistical inferences. A consistent approach to model selection, more and more likely, arrives at the correct decision about the best model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  * 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            M 
                          as the sample size  n  grows. When an inconsistent approach to model selection procedure is used, then as as the sample size  n  grows, the data analyst will be more and more convinced about the wrong answer about which model from

With respect to the second point, it turns out that approaches #1, #2, #3, and #4 assume a particular estimator of the true sampling density that arises as a particular case of the Bayesian nonparametric model selection procedure #5, as discussed in detail towards the end of this section.

                         . For virtually all parametric models, the Bayes factor provides an asymptotically consistent approach to model selection. However see  Berger et al. Moreover, the Bayes factor has a practical limitation in that it is very difficult to compute for many high-dimensional parametric models. Also, the Bayes factor is undefined when one of the models is specified under a prior  
                            f 
                            ( 
                            θ 
                            )

                         ). This last issue is critical. While “non-subjective” priors are often implemented in statistical practice (because it can be an expensive undertaking to elicit proper subjective priors), these priors are usually improper (e.g.,  Yang & Berger, 1997 ).

Cross-validation is the simplest approach to model selection, and there are classical-frequentist and Bayesian versions of this approach (Classical cross-validation:  Mosier, 1951; Allen, 1974; Browne, 2000; Geisser, 1975; Geisser & Eddy, 1979; Hastie et al., 2001; Stone, 1974 ; Bayesian cross-validation:  Berger & Pericchi, 1996a, b, 1998 ;  Key et al., 1999; Lampinen & Vehtari, 2002; Vehtari & Lampinen, 2003 ; for a similar approach, see  O’Hagan, 1995 ).                             i 
                            ∈

                            1 
                            , 
                            …

                            } 
                            ⊆ 
                            { 
                            1 
                            , 
                            …

                            } 
                            }

                                  d

A third approach to model selection is provided by any one of several so-called penalty criteria, where each criterion is based on the utility function of the form

It is known that Akaike's Information Criterion (AIC,  Akaike, 1973 ) is asymptotically equivalent to cross-validation in decisions on model choice ( Stone, 1977 ), that the Bayesian information Criterion (BIC,  Schwarz, 1978 ) provides an asymptotic approximation to the Bayes factor, and that the Deviance Information Criterion (DIC,  Spiegelhalter et al., 2002 ) provides a Bayesian analog to the AIC. However, all three criteria are not asymptotically consistent procedures of model choice ( Berger et al., 2003; Spiegelhalter et al., 2002; Stone, 1979 ), and the DIC is not invariant over one-to-one transformations of the model parameters. Extensions of AIC and BIC have been proposed (for example, see  Berger et al., 2003; Bozdogan, 2000 ), and there also exists Bayesian predictive criteria where the model penalty  
                            
                               
                                  P 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                          is defined by a weighted sum of the variance of the predictions over the  n  observations (see  Gelfand & Ghosh, 1998; Laud & Ibrahim, 1995 ). The Minimum Description Length (MDL) criterion ( Grünwald, 2004a,b ;  Rissanen, 2001 ) identifies the best model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  * 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            M 
                          as the one having the highest value of the fraction  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  nd

                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                            / 
                            ∫ 
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                                  ( 
                                  y 
                                  ) 
                                  d

                                  n

                                  1

To address this practical issue, an easier-to-calculate asymptotic approximation of the MDL ( Rissanen, 1996 ) is usually implemented. However this approximation can dramatically fail for even simple models analyzed on an impractically large sample size  n  ( Navarro, 2004 ).

While the bootstrap approach is asymptotically equivalent to AIC and leave-one-out cross-validation ( Efron, 1983 ), they all share the same deficiency that they are asymptotically inconsistent approaches to model selection ( Shao, 1996 ). More generally,  Schervish (1996, Section 5.3; 1995)  proves that the success of the bootstrap highly depends on the accuracy of the empirical-distribution estimator

While  
                            p 
                         -values are ubiquitous in areas of applied statistics,  
                            p 
                         -values are associated with a very long history of criticisms (see for example,  Bayarri & Berger, 2004; Berger & Sellke, 1987; Berkson, 1938 ;  Bernardo & Smith, 1994 , Appendix B.3.3 and B.3.4;  Bernardo & Rueda, 2002; Edwards et al., 1963; Good, 1983; Jaynes, 2003; Jeffreys, 1961, 1990; Matthews, 2001; Pratt, 1961; Schervish, 1996 ; and references therein). Seven of the main criticisms of  
                            p 
                         -values are as follows. 
                            
                               1. 
                               
                                  Incoherence : The  
                                     p 
                                  -value is ambiguous, because in most practical settings the sample space  
                                     X 
                                     ⊆ 
                                     
                                        
                                           R 
                                        
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                     
                                   is ill-defined ( Lindley, 1999 ). In particular, given a set of data  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                  , it is possible to produce a range of  
                                     p 
                                  -values by simply varying the sample space. Also, while it is possible to formulate a likelihood ratio test of a null hypothesis  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                     : 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           nd 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                   against the alternative  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     : 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           nd 
                                        
                                     
                                     ≠ 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                  , where the goal is to minimize Type II error  
                                     
                                        
                                           β 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̲ 
                                        
                                     
                                   for some fixed Type I error  
                                     
                                        
                                           α 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̲ 
                                        
                                     
                                   rate (as in the Neyman–Pearson approach to testing;  Neyman & Pearson, 1933, 1967 ), the  only  way to make coherent decisions with these error rates, is to base decisions on minimizing some  linear combination  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           w 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           α 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̲ 
                                        
                                     
                                     + 
                                     
                                        
                                           w 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           β 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̲ 
                                        
                                     
                                   ( Lindley, 1972 ). 
                            
                            
                               2. 
                               
                                  Arbitrary decision rules : Conventional cut-off points for  
                                     p 
                                  -values like 0.05, are arbitrary, and ignore power, the dimensionality of the problem, and the sample size. Moreover, “Even the most statistically apathetic must have wondered precisely why a  
                                     p 
                                  -value of 0.049 is deemed ‘statistically significant’, while one of 0.051 is not” ( Matthews, 2001 , p. 45). 
                            
                            
                               3. 
                               
                                  Statistical versus practical significance : On the basis of a  
                                     p 
                                  -value from a test of the null hypothesis  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                     : 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           nd 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                   against the alternative  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     : 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           nd 
                                        
                                     
                                     ≠ 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                  , there is no way to formally assess whether the (unknown) true sampling density  
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                   is  practically  different from the sampling density  
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           nd 
                                        
                                     
                                   predicted by a model  
                                     
                                        
                                           M 
                                        
                                        
                                           d 
                                        
                                     
                                  . This is because the classical theory of hypothesis testing does not make use of any utility function. Furthermore, it is long known (e.g.,  Berkson, 1938 ) that testing the exact truth of a sharp statistical hypothesis (e.g.,  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                     : 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           nd 
                                        
                                     
                                  ) is rarely appropriate, since almost every model used in practice is known not to be strictly correct. So: “Because of this, although a typical statistical test with a small sample will rarely reject, for large enough samples it will nearly always reject. Consequently, whether or not a test rejects a null hypothesis appears to have more to do with the sample size than with  how true  a null hypothesis is” ( Kadane & Dickey, 1980 , p. 46). 
                            
                            
                               4. 
                               
                                  Contradictions : When testing point-null hypotheses in a multivariate model with parameter

                               5. 
                               
                                  Not a direct measure of evidence : In general, there is no direct mathematical relation between the  
                                     p 
                                  -value and  
                                     Pr

                                     p

                                     21 
                                  
                                  
                                     21 
                                     The likelihood principle is a basic axiom of statistical inference ( Birnbaum, 1962 ) which requires that all the information about the data

                                        ). 
                                   and thus can yield paradoxical results even in very simple hypothesis-testing procedures (for example, see  Lindley & Phillips, 1976 ). 
                            
                            
                               6. 
                               
                                  Exaggeration in point-null hypothesis testing : Since the  
                                     p 
                                  -value averages over unobserved data

                               7. 
                               
                                  Non-uniformity in composite-null hypothesis testing : In model (or hypothesis) testing problems, where  
                                     θ

                                     θ 
                                  , or at least asymptotically Uniform(0,1). This uniformity is important because it allows  
                                     p 
                                  -values have a common interpretation across different statistical problems. However, when the test statistic  T  is not ancillary (or approximately so), which is usually the case in practical settings,  
                                     p 
                                  -values can be far from uniform, both asymptotically ( Robins et al., 2000 ), and for finite samples ( Bayarri & Berger, 2000, 2004 ). In particular, the conditional plug-in  
                                     p

                                           [ 
                                           cMLE

Arbitrary decision rules : Conventional cut-off points for  
                                     p 
                                  -values like 0.05, are arbitrary, and ignore power, the dimensionality of the problem, and the sample size. Moreover, “Even the most statistically apathetic must have wondered precisely why a  
                                     p 
                                  -value of 0.049 is deemed ‘statistically significant’, while one of 0.051 is not” ( Matthews, 2001 , p. 45).
