                                  , or vice versa ( Healy, 1969; Rao, 1966 ). Also, for some statistical tests of significance, it is possible to reject the null hypothesis  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                     : 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           nd 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                   at the 0.01 level of significance, and not reject the same null hypothesis at the 0.05 level (for more technical details, see  Chernoff, 1951; Stein, 1951 ).

Not a direct measure of evidence : In general, there is no direct mathematical relation between the  
                                     p 
                                  -value and  
                                     Pr 
                                     [ 
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                     | 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ] 
                                  , the probability that the null is true given the evidence. This is because the  
                                     p 
                                  -value is not fully conditioned on the available evidence  
                                     T 
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                     = 
                                     T 
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  , but instead is averaged over a set of less-relevant events  
                                     { 
                                     T 
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                     ⩾ 
                                     T 
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                     } 
                                  , with respect to the model likelihood  
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           d 
                                        
                                     
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                     | 
                                     
                                     θ 
                                     ) 
                                   and all possible data sets  
                                     { 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     } 
                                   that include  unobserved  data sets. Thus the null hypothesis  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                   “ 
                                     … 
                                   may be rejected because it has not predicted observable results which have not occurred” ( Jeffreys, 1980 , p. 453). Furthermore, since the  
                                     p 
                                  -value is averaged over unobserved data, it violates the Likelihood Principle, 
                                     21 
                                  
                                  
                                     21 
                                     The likelihood principle is a basic axiom of statistical inference ( Birnbaum, 1962 ) which requires that all the information about the data  
                                           
                                              
                                                 x 
                                              
                                              
                                                 n 
                                              
                                           
                                         should be summarized by the model's likelihood  
                                           
                                              
                                                 f 
                                              
                                              
                                                 d 
                                              
                                           
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 x 
                                              
                                              
                                                 n 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                           | 
                                           
                                           θ 
                                           ) 
                                         (or more generally,  
                                           
                                              
                                                 f 
                                              
                                              
                                                 d 
                                              
                                           
                                           ( 
                                           
                                              
                                                 x 
                                              
                                              
                                                 n 
                                              
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        ). 
                                   and thus can yield paradoxical results even in very simple hypothesis-testing procedures (for example, see  Lindley & Phillips, 1976 ).

Exaggeration in point-null hypothesis testing : Since the  
                                     p 
                                  -value averages over unobserved data  
                                     { 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     } 
                                     ⧹ 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                  , it exaggerates the evidence against a point-null hypothesis  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                  . For example, a  
                                     p 
                                  -value of 0.05 implies  at most  3:1 odds against  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                   ( Berger & Delampady, 1987; Berger & Sellke, 1987 ), while under the prior probability  
                                     Pr 
                                     [ 
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                     ] 
                                     = 
                                     0.99 
                                  , a  
                                     p 
                                  -value of 0.05 implies a posterior probability  
                                     Pr 
                                     [ 
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                     | 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ] 
                                   of at least 0.96 ( Matthews, 2001 ).

Non-uniformity in composite-null hypothesis testing : In model (or hypothesis) testing problems, where  
                                     θ 
                                   is unknown and not fixed on a specific hypothesized value  
                                     
                                        
                                           θ 
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 H 
                                              
                                              
                                                 d 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  , it is critically important for the  
                                     p 
                                  -value to be Uniform(0,1) under the null hypothesis  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                   for all values of a model parameter  
                                     θ 
                                  , or at least asymptotically Uniform(0,1). This uniformity is important because it allows  
                                     p 
                                  -values have a common interpretation across different statistical problems. However, when the test statistic  T  is not ancillary (or approximately so), which is usually the case in practical settings,  
                                     p 
                                  -values can be far from uniform, both asymptotically ( Robins et al., 2000 ), and for finite samples ( Bayarri & Berger, 2000, 2004 ). In particular, the conditional plug-in  
                                     p 
                                  -value is asymptotically anti-conservative (overly rejects  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                  ) because it is fully conditioned on the MLE  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 θ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ^ 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           d 
                                           [ 
                                           cMLE 
                                           ] 
                                        
                                     
                                  , and thus does not fully account for the uncertainty in  
                                     θ 
                                  . The plug-in, posterior predictive, and discrepancy predictive  
                                     p 
                                  -values are asymptotically very conservative (under-detects departures from  
                                     
                                        
                                           H 
                                        
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                     
                                  ). This is because in the calculation of any of these three  
                                     p 
                                  -values, the data  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                   are being used twice 
                                     22 
                                  
                                  
                                     22 
                                     The prior-predictive  
                                           p 
                                        -value of  Box (1980)  does not use the data twice, because it is averaged over the prior density  
                                           
                                              
                                                 f 
                                              
                                              
                                                 d 
                                              
                                           
                                           ( 
                                           θ 
                                           ) 
                                        , not over the full posterior density  
                                           
                                              
                                                 f 
                                              
                                              
                                                 d 
                                              
                                           
                                           ( 
                                           θ 
                                           
                                           | 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 x 
                                              
                                              
                                                 n 
                                              
                                           
                                           ) 
                                        . 
                                  , first to construct the estimate of the the parameter (e.g., the MLE  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 θ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ^ 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           d 
                                        
                                     
                                   or the posterior  
                                     
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                        
                                           d 
                                        
                                     
                                     ( 
                                     θ 
                                     
                                     | 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  ), and then once again to compute the tail area of the distribution of the test statistic  
                                     T 
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                   that exceeds  
                                     T 
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  .

The sufficient statistic, prior predictive, partial posterior predictive, and conditional predictive  
                            p 
                         -values are asymptotically uniform(0,1) ( Robins et al., 2000 ), with the prior-predictive  
                            p 
                         -value being marginally uniform in an average sense over  
                            θ 
                          ( Bayarri & Berger, 2000 ). Unfortunately these four  
                            p 
                         -values have nontrivial practical limitations. The computation of the sufficient-statistic  
                            p 
                         -value can be burdensome, and sufficient statistics do not exist in many practical problems ( Bayarri & Berger, 2000 ). The prior predictive  
                            p 
                         -value is undefined when the prior  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                            ( 
                            θ 
                            ) 
                          is improper. This is an important shortcoming, because nonsubjective priors are very often used in practical settings, and such priors are typically improper (e.g.,  Yang & Berger, 1997 ). The partial posterior predictive and conditional predictive  
                            p 
                         -values can be very difficult to compute ( Pauler, 1999 ). Even when such practical limitations can be overcome, to yield a  
                            p 
                         -value that is asymptotically Uniform(0,1), the other six issues of  
                            p 
                         -values still remain.

Given the above well-known issues with  
                            p 
                         -values, it seems worth exploring an alternative approach to model testing, such as the Bayesian nonparametric procedure for model testing presented in  Section 2.3 . This approach to model testing is based on Bayesian decision theory, and is thus consistent with the axioms of quantitative coherence (for example, the procedure satisfies the Likelihood Principle and avoids using the data twice). Moreover the procedure directly measures the evidence of a model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          with an intuitively-sensible, posterior-expected utility  
                            
                               
                                  u 
                               
                               
                                  ¯ 
                               
                            
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                            
                            | 
                            
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                          that measures the amount of information that the model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          (via  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  nd 
                               
                            
                         ) describes in the (unknown) true sampling density  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         . This utility can also be used as a basis for judging the practical significance of the difference  
                            
                               
                                  u 
                               
                               
                                  ¯ 
                               
                            
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  D 
                               
                            
                            
                            | 
                            
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                            - 
                            
                               
                                  u 
                               
                               
                                  ¯ 
                               
                            
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                            
                            | 
                            
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                            ⩾ 
                            0 
                          for any arbitrary-large sample size  n , such that the decision is made to reject a simplified model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            { 
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                            
                               
                                  } 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  D 
                                  - 
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          if that difference exceeds some critical value  
                            
                               
                                  u 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                               
                                  * 
                               
                            
                         . As mentioned, this critical value reflects the maximum amount of information the decision-maker (the statistician) is willing to lose about the true model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  D 
                               
                            
                          in order to work with a simplified model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          (that is possibly incorrect).

In summary, the model testing approach answers the question: “Is it useful to assume that the model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          (that predicts a sampling density  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  nd 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                          provides an adequate description of the (unknown) true  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         ?” This appears to be a more realistic question to pose, than the question: “Does the model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          predict the sampling density  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                          exactly?”, which is answered by the traditional approach to model testing that involves a  
                            p 
                         -value calculated from the test of the null hypothesis  
                            
                               
                                  H 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            : 
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  nd 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         , against the alternative  
                            
                               
                                  H 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            : 
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  nd 
                               
                            
                            ≠ 
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         . It is been known for a long time that testing the exact truth of a null hypothesis is hardly ever appropriate (e.g.,  Berger & Delampady, 1987; Berkson, 1938; Kadane & Dickey, 1980 ), because any parametric (or deterministic) model is known not to be strictly correct. As mentioned in the earlier quote of  Kadane & Dickey (1980) , it is well-known that the null hypothesis  
                            
                               
                                  H 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            : 
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  nd 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                          can be rejected for any arbitrary Type I error rate, even when  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  nd 
                               
                            
                          explains almost all of the information in the true sampling density  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         .

This section discusses applications of the Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model testing, on four real data sets arising from psychological research. The Pólya Tree prior will be used in these applications. This is a general nonparametric prior that includes the well-known Dirichlet Process prior as a special case. Before proceeding to these applications, it is necessary to provide some background about this nonparametric prior.

Finally, while the construction of the Pólya Tree prior was illustrated in the modelling of data from a univariate sample space  
                         X 
                       
                      
                         ⊆ 
                         R 
                      , a Pólya Tree prior can also be constructed 
                         24 
                      
                      
                         24 
                         The Pólya Tree prior can be constructed for a sample space  
                               X 
                               ⊂ 
                               
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                               
                             of any arbitrary finite dimension  
                               p 
                               ∈ 
                               { 
                               1 
                               , 
                               2 
                               , 
                               3 
                               , 
                               … 
                               } 
                            . In this more general case, there are  
                               
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                               
                             splits per level  j  of the Polya Tree, and each of the “bin” probabilities  
                               
                                  
                                     C 
                                  
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                               
                             is assumed to be a Dirichlet random variable (instead of a Beta random variable). Of course this Dirichlet distribution is updated after a set of data  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                             is observed. The posterior predictive distribution  
                               
                                  
                                     F 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                             is then calculated in the same manner as in Eq.  (33) . See Paddock ( Paddock, 1999; Paddock et al., 2003 ) for more technical details about the multivariate case. 
                       on any multivariate sample space  
                         X 
                         ⊆ 
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               p 
                            
                         
                       in the same manner.

In the application of the Pólya Tree prior, it is convenient to specify  
                         
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               ε 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                       whenever a binary sequence  
                         ε 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               ε 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         ⋯ 
                         
                            
                               ε 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                       defines a set  
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               ε 
                            
                         
                       at level  j , where  
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                       denotes a nonnegative constant that depends on  j  ( Walker et al., 1999 ). Furthermore, when it is the belief that the true  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       is a continuous density, it is then desirable for the top levels ( j  small) for  
                         F 
                         ( 
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⋯ 
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                     - 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               0 
                            
                         
                         ) 
                       and  
                         F 
                         ( 
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⋯ 
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                     - 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               1 
                            
                         
                         ) 
                       to have a large amount of variability, while  
                         F 
                         ( 
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⋯ 
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                     - 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               0 
                            
                         
                         ) 
                       and  
                         F 
                         ( 
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⋯ 
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                     - 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               1 
                            
                         
                         ) 
                       should be “close” for lower levels of the tree ( j  large). This can be achieved by allowing  
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                       to increase with  j , for example by setting  
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               cj 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                      , where the constant  
                         c 
                         > 
                         0 
                       is a tuning parameter that represents the amount of prior information relative to sample size ( Lavine, 1992; Ferguson, 1974 ). According to  Lavine (1992) , the specification  
                         c 
                         = 
                         1 
                       leading to  
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               j 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       for all  j , provides “a sensible canonical choice” of Pólya Tree prior. This choice of prior corresponds to the belief that the distribution  F  is absolutely continuous with probability 1 ( Draghici & Ramamoorthi, 2000; Ferguson, 1974 ). Since  
                         c 
                         = 
                         1 
                       is “small” then Lavine's prior may be viewed as implying a rather nonsubjective prior. Moreover, the choice of prior  
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               j 
                            
                            
                               3 
                               + 
                               η 
                            
                         
                      , for some  
                         η 
                         > 
                         0 
                      , is sufficient for posterior consistency (and gives full support to absolutely continuous densities), such that  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         E 
                         [ 
                         F 
                         
                         | 
                         
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         ] 
                         
                         ( 
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         E 
                         [ 
                         f 
                         
                         | 
                         
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         ] 
                      , respectively) converges to the true sampling distribution  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       (true sampling density  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                      , respectively) as  
                         n 
                         → 
                         ∞ 
                       (see  Walker, 2004b , Section 6.2). (The previous statement implies that the Levine's prior  
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               j 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       is not sufficient for consistency.) Note that either choice of Pólya Tree prior (namely  
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               cj 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       for  
                         c 
                         > 
                         0 
                      , or  
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               j 
                            
                            
                               3 
                               + 
                               η 
                            
                         
                       for some  
                         η 
                         > 
                         0 
                      ) can be viewed as “non-subjective” in the sense that, for every binary sequence  
                         ε 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               ε 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         ⋯ 
                         
                            
                               ε 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                       on any level  j  of the Pólya Tree, each prior assumes that 0.5 is the prior-predictive probability than an observation falls in either “bin”  
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⋯ 
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                     - 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               0 
                            
                         
                       or  
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⋯ 
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                     - 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               1 
                            
                         
                       (in particular,  
                         
                            
                               α 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⋯ 
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                     - 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               0 
                            
                         
                         / 
                         ( 
                         
                            
                               α 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⋯

                               1

                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⋯ 
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                     -

                               0

There are at least two well-known special cases of the Pólya Tree prior. First, the specification  
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         c 
                         / 
                         
                            
                               2 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                      , for any  
                         c 
                         > 
                         0 
                       and all  j , corresponds to the Dirichlet Process prior. This prior has the property that  
                         
                            
                               c 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                         → 
                         0 
                       as  
                         j 
                         → 
                         ∞ 
                      , which is the wrong direction if it is believed that the true  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       is a continuous density ( Walker et al., 1999 ). Thus the Dirichlet Process prior assigns probability 1 to the space of discrete probability distributions ( Blackwell, 1973; Walker et al., 1999 ). It is worth noting that the Dirichlet Process prior has a counter-intuitive property, in that it assumes that negative correlation exists between the probabilities  
                         F 
                         ( 
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               h 
                            
                         
                         ) 
                       and  
                         F 
                         ( 
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               l 
                            
                         
                         ) 
                      , for any pair of partitions  
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               h 
                            
                         
                         ≠ 
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               l 
                            
                         
                       of a sample space  
                         X 
                         ⊆ 
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               p 
                            
                         
                       ( Walker et al., 1999 ). Second, it can be shown that a nonsubjective prior is defined in the limit as  
                         
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               ε 
                            
                         
                         → 
                         0 
                       for all binary sequences  
                         ε 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               ε 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         ⋯ 
                         
                            
                               ε 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                      , such that the resulting posterior estimate  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                       ( 
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                      , respectively) of the true sampling distribution  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                      
                      (true sampling density  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                      , respectively) is the the empirical distribution function  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               ^ 
                            
                         
                       that assigns probability  
                         
                            
                               
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         
                       to each observation  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                      . Recall that in the limit of a Dirichlet Process prior  
                         α 
                         ( 
                         X 
                         ) 
                         → 
                         0 
                      , the resulting posterior  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                       is the empirical distribution  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               ^ 
                            
                         
                      . Moreover, the empirical distribution  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               ^ 
                            
                         
                      
                      providesthe basis for the classical bootstrap ( Efron, 1979 ), the Bayesian bootstrap ( Rubin, 1981 ), and the Empirical Likelihood approach (e.g.,  Owen, 2001 ).

In theory, the Pólya Tree prior models a continuous density with an infinite number of levels of the tree, such that at the bottom of this tree  
                         ( 
                         j 
                         = 
                         ∞ 
                         ) 
                      , each bin  
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ⋯ 
                               
                                  
                                     ε 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∞ 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                       is infinitely small, to the point where either the prior or posterior probability that an observation  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               n 
                               + 
                               1 
                            
                         
                       falls in the bin is zero (see  Lavine, 1992 ). However in practice, it is only possible to work with a finite number of levels of the Pólya Tree. So then, when modelling a continuous random variable  X , the idea is to construct a Pólya Tree with a “large enough” number of levels. The present author has written a program that can be used to perform full prior and posterior inference with the Pólya Tree having 10 levels. This results in  
                         
                            
                               2 
                            
                            
                               10 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         1024 
                       bins at the bottom of this tree, each bin of size  
                         1 
                         / 
                         
                            
                               2 
                            
                            
                               10 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         0.000977 
                       according to a sample space  
                         X 
                         = 
                         [ 
                         0 
                         , 
                         1 
                         ] 
                       (which can be transformed to any single-dimensional sample space of interest, such as on the reals  
                         X 
                         = 
                         R 
                      , or on the positive reals  
                         X 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               + 
                            
                         
                      ).

With the Pólya Tree prior described, along with its relations to the empirical distribution and the Dirichlet Process prior, the following subsections proceed to discuss the four applications of the Bayesian Nonparametric approach to model selection and model testing, on four real data sets. It is important to note that although the present discussion has focused on the Pólya Tree prior and the special case of the Dirichlet Process prior, in principle, the Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model testing applies to any type of nonparametric prior distribution  
                         Π 
                       over the space of densities  
                         
                            
                               Ω 
                            
                            
                               X 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         { 
                         f 
                         ( 
                         · 
                         ) 
                         
                            
                               } 
                            
                            
                               X 
                            
                         
                      .

Weber & Hsee (1998)  conducted an experiment where they considered 12 different investment options as stimuli. For each stimulus, a subject judged the amount of money (in American dollars) s/he believed that the option was worth, and also judged the riskiness of the option. Over all 12 investment options, 31 German subjects made 724 responses, and 87 American subjects made 2087 responses. Also, every subject made two repeated judgments on each investment option. Here, this experiment is viewed as a  
                            2 
                            × 
                            3 
                          factorial design, as presented in  Table 5 
                         . For example, among the 340 total responses where Germans (G) judged investment options to be medium risk  
                            ( 
                            M 
                            ) 
                         , the term  
                            
                               
                                  F 
                               
                               
                                  GM 
                               
                            
                          denotes the distribution of the amount of money (in American dollars) that Germans are willing to pay for those investment options (that is,  
                            
                               
                                  F 
                               
                               
                                  GM 
                               
                            
                          is a distribution of willingness to pay, based on the 340 responses where German subjects judged any one of the 12 investment options to be medium risk, in any one of the repeated trials). Each of the six distributions from the  
                            2 
                            × 
                            3 
                          factorial design is defined on the sample space  
                            X 
                            = 
                            [ 
                            0 
                            , 
                            ∞ 
                            ) 
                          of American dollars.

It is of interest to evaluate whether the six population distributions follow a stochastic order, while avoiding the assumption that these distributions are governed by any parametric form whatsoever.

The results of the Bayesian nonparametric model selection and model test analysis are summarized in  Table 6 
                         , for each of the nine stochastic ordering models, and the true model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  D 
                               
                            
                         . According to the results of the Kullback–Liebler divergence in  Table 6 , the best stochastic ordering model is the one represented by the posterior CDFs already given in  Fig. 5 . Also, according to the critical value of  
                            0.09 
                         , this model is the only stochastic ordering model that is not rejected as a poor description of the true sampling density  
                            
                               
                                  F 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                          (true sampling density  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         ). In summary, from the best-fitting stochastic ordering model (model  
                            
                               
                                  M 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                         ), we learn that within each of the three levels of perceived risk, Germans are willing to pay more for investment options than Americans are, and that within each nationality, willingness to pay increases as the perceived risk of the investment option decreases.

At this point it is natural to examine the sensitivity of the Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model selection, to different choices of Pólya Tree prior. In the present data analysis we considered  Lavine's (1992)  “sensible canonical choice” of prior given by

                            …

                         ). Recall that the last two priors provide sufficient conditions for posterior consistency (see  Walker, 2004a ).  Fig. 6 
                          compares the Kullback–Leibler divergence of the nine stochastic-order models according to each of the four different choices of Pólya Tree prior. Notice that, in terms of model selection, the preference-order of the nine models remains unchanged. Moreover, in terms of model testing, Model 1 is not rejected under any of the four choices of Pólya Tree prior (for the critical value of 0.09), while Models 2 and 3 are not rejected under either priors
