                         . Thus the decisions on model selection and model testing are quite stable over the four different choices of Pólya Tree prior.

Here we analyze a set of data arising from a reality-monitoring experiment, reported in  Harvey (1985) . This experiment involved the study of Schizophrenic subjects with a thought disorder, and used a mixed list of two groups of words, namely “Say” words and “Think” words. In the study period of the experiment, every subject was instructed to speak each of the Say words out loud, and to quietly think each of the Think words. After the study period, each subject was then presented with a different mixed list of words. This new list contained the same Say and Think words, but also containing new words. For each word in this new list, each subject was then instructed to correctly indicate whether the word was a Say word, Think word, or a New word. The data in  Table 7 
                          summarizes the responses made by the schizophrenic subjects, by word type (obtained from  Harvey, 1985 ).

In clinical psychology it has been suggested that reality-monitoring failures contribute to schizophrenia (e.g.,  Johnson, 1988 ). With this idea,  Batchelder & Riefer (1990)  proposed analyzing these data with a cognitive model, a version of the Multinomial Processing Tree model (see  Batchelder & Riefer, 1999 , for a review), which assumes that the three sets of multinomial responses (one per row, or  Word Type , in  Table 7 ) are governed by latent parameters  
                            θ 
                            = 
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  1

                                  θ

                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  3 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  4 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  5 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  6 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  7 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                            ∈ 
                            [ 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1

Transitivity is a basic axiom of most behavioral theories of decision-making. Let  
                            ≽ 
                          denote preference or indifference. Transitivity predicts that decision-maker  always  makes preferences according to the rule: 
                            
                               (58) 
                               
                                  a 
                                  ≽ 
                                  b 
                                  
                                  and 
                                  
                                  b 
                                  ≽ 
                                  c 
                                  
                                  implies 
                                  
                                  a 
                                  ≽ 
                                  c 
                                  
                                  for all 
                                  
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  , 
                                  c 
                                  ∈ 
                                  S 
                                  .

Clearly, the axiom in Eq.  (58)  is deterministic, and does not account for the randomness that appears to be inherent in real choice behavior. Therefore probabilistic versions of transitivity have been formulated to account for such randomness. A flexible version of deterministic transitivity is called Weak Stochastic Transitivity (WST), which predicts that 
                            
                               (59) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        θ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ab 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⩾ 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                  and 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        θ 
                                     
                                     
                                        bc 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⩾ 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                  implies 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        θ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ac 
                                     
                                  
                                  ⩾ 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  for all 
                                  
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  , 
                                  c 
                                  ∈ 
                                  S 
                                  , 
                               
                            
                         holds, where for any pair  
                            { 
                            a 
                            , 
                            b 
                            } 
                         ,  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  ab 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            Pr 
                            [ 
                            a 
                            ≻ 
                            b 
                            ] 
                          denotes a choice probability parameter (see  Block & Marschak, 1960 ).

                         under the constraints of Weak Stochastic Transitivity (Eq.  (59) ), calculated a  
                            p 
                         -value from a  
                            
                               
                                  χ 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                          test under the null hypothesis of WST, and then concluded that the subjects violated WST.  Iverson & Falmagne (1985)  reanalyzed Tversky's data, using better, maximum-likelihood estimates (MLEs) of the 10 choice probabilities. They calculated a  
                            p 
                         -value according to a likelihood ratio test under the null of WST, and found that seven of the eight subjects failed to violate WST. Given the many aforementioned issues with  
                            p 
                         -values, the present writer hesitates to draw any definite conclusions from the conflicting results between the studies of  Tversky (1969)  and  Iverson & Falmagne (1985) .

Here, Tversky's data is reanalyzed using the Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model testing. In particular, two parametric models are considered, the first representing WST (Eq.  (59) ) and the second representing deterministic transitivity (Eq.  (58) ). For the WST model, we use  Iverson & Falmagne's (1985)  MLEs of all the choice parameter vectors

In general, the results of this analysis supports Tversky's hypothesis that it is possible for decision-makers to violate transitivity in their preferences.

27 
                            The results presented in this section arises from the collaboration of the present author, with his student Xin Liu on her working dissertation ( Liu, 2005 ). 
                          In the practice of Item Response Theory (IRT), with a data set arising from  n  persons who each respond to a  L -item test (or attitude survey), the primary goal is to infer a unidimensional latent trait  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            R 
                          of each person, that trait reflecting his/her level of ability on the test (or level of agreement on the attitude survey). The vast majority of IRT models are “strictly unidimensional”, in the sense that they assume that exactly one unidimensional latent trait  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            R 
                         
                         governs a person's responses on an  L -item test. However, this assumption of strict unidimensionality may be viewed as overly-restrictive for real psychometric practice. For example, it is easy to imagine that a person's success on a mathematics exam depends on at least four latent traits, namely her mathematics ability, reasoning ability, verbal ability, and memory. More generally, it can be very difficult to construct a test that measures exactly one latent trait, because often multiple latent traits in a person triggers his/her response to a single test item.

To address such practical issues in psychometrics,  Stout (1987, 1990)  proposed the Essential Unidimensionality (EU) model of IRT, which generalizes all the strictly unidimensional IRT models. In particular, the EU model allows for many correlated unidimensional latent traits  
                            { 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  3 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  4 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            … 
                            } 
                          to govern a person's responses to items on a test, where  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          measures a level of a dominant latent trait (e.g., math ability), and  
                            { 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  3 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  4 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            … 
                            } 
                          measure subtraits of the dominant latent trait (e.g., reasoning ability, verbal ability, and memory).

Let  
                            X 
                            = 
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  X

                          denote the random variable that represents the responses to a set of  L  test items  
                            ℓ 
                            =

                            L 
                         . Also, let an observation

In other words, we conclude that the six items of the NAEP reading test satisfy essential unidimensionality, in the sense that, the 6-item test measures one dominant latent trait

Note that the same exact procedure illustrated in  Eq. (68)  can be applied to test the fit of the EU model on data involving any number  
                            L 
                            ⩾ 
                            2 
                          of test items scored on either on a discrete or on a continuous scale (or any mixture of both).

This article presented a Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model testing, that implements ideas from Bayesian decision theory and information theory. The approach is useful for evaluating a set of models that are either parametric or deterministic, such that each model can either be analyzed from a Bayesian or a classical-frequentist approach to statistical inference. Given an observed set of data  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                      , the Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection identifies the single model  
                         
                            
                               M 
                            
                            
                               d 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         M 
                       that that predicts a sampling density  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               nd 
                            
                         
                       that explains the most information about the truth  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                      , while the approach to model testing determines whether that model's prediction  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               nd 
                            
                         
                       explains enough information about the true density  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       (this truth estimated by  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         E 
                         [ 
                         f 
                         
                         | 
                         
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         ] 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               nD 
                            
                         
                      ).

The Bayesian nonparametric approach seems to compare favorably to all other four types of model selection methods, in that the approach provides a consistent procedure for model choice, and can be applied to evaluate the fit to models specified under in improper prior (provided that the resulting posterior is proper). Also, the Bayesian nonparametric estimator  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         E 
                         [ 
                         f 
                         
                         | 
                         
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                         ] 
                       of the true  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       can be more realistic than the empirical-distribution estimator assumed by any of the other four types of model selection procedures. The Bayesian nonparametric approach also compares favorably to the traditional approach to model testing based on the  
                         p 
                      -value, as it provides a utility-based method to define practical significance explicitly in terms of information units, and it avoids the many fundamental statistical issues that are associated with  
                         p 
                      -values. Finally, as demonstrated in the four real data-analysis examples of the previous section, the Bayesian nonparametric approach to model selection and model rejection can be easy implemented to evaluate the fit of models on real data.
